questions.question: Explain the advantages of implementing stacks using linked lists over array-based implementations.
keyPoints: Dynamic size: can grow and shrink at runtime unlike fixed-size arrays
No overflow: limited only by available heap memory, not a predefined capacity
Memory efficient: allocates memory only when needed, no wasted space
All operations (push, pop, peek) remain O(1) time complexity

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement a stack using linked list with push, pop, peek, isEmpty, and display operations.
keyPoints: Define StackNode structure with int data and struct StackNode* next
Initialize top pointer to NULL for empty stack
Push: allocate node with malloc, set newNode->next = top, update top = newNode
Pop: check if top==NULL (underflow), save data, update top = top->next, free old node
Peek: return top->data without modification after checking if stack is empty
isEmpty: return top == NULL

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement a queue using linked list with enqueue, dequeue, front, rear, isEmpty, and display operations. Explain the significance of maintaining both front and rear pointers.
keyPoints: Define QueueNode structure with data and next pointer
Maintain two pointers: front (for deletion/dequeue) and rear (for insertion/enqueue)
Enqueue: create node, if queue empty set both front and rear to newNode, else rear->next = newNode and rear = newNode
Dequeue: check if front==NULL (underflow), save front->data, move front = front->next, if front becomes NULL set rear to NULL, free old node
Front and Rear: return respective node data after checking if queue is empty
Two pointers ensure O(1) for both enqueue and dequeue operations

questions.question: Compare and contrast array-based and linked list-based implementations of stacks and queues with respect to memory allocation, time complexity, and practical considerations.
keyPoints: Array: fixed size declared at compile time; Linked list: dynamic size using malloc/free at runtime
Array: possible overflow when capacity reached; Linked list: no overflow unless system runs out of heap memory
Array: contiguous memory better for cache performance; Linked list: nodes scattered, pointer overhead per node
Both implementations: push/pop/enqueue/dequeue are O(1), traversal/display is O(n)
Array: simpler implementation with no memory leaks; Linked: requires careful malloc checking and free to prevent leaks
Choose linked list when size is unpredictable; choose array when maximum size is known and memory is constrained

questions.question: Explain the conditions that lead to stack underflow and queue underflow in linked list implementations. How are these conditions detected and handled?
keyPoints: Stack underflow: occurs when attempting to pop from an empty stack (top == NULL)
Queue underflow: occurs when attempting to dequeue from an empty queue (front == NULL)
Detection: check if top == NULL before pop, check if front == NULL before dequeue
Handling: print error message, return sentinel value like INT_MIN, do not attempt to access node->data
